description: Add streaming progress support for long-running tool operations. Currently, tool dispatch uses a request/response pattern with a hard 30s timeout (DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS in state.ts). Long-running tools (e.g., bulk data exports, search-and-aggregate operations) cannot report intermediate progress, and the 30s ceiling forces tools to be fast or fail. This PRD adds a progress callback to the tool handler context (backward-compatible), wires progress notifications from the browser adapter through the extension WebSocket to the MCP server, converts them to MCP ProgressNotifications (notifications/progress with progressToken), makes the dispatch timeout reset on progress (the tool is alive), and validates the full chain with E2E tests.

userStories.title: Add progress callback to tool handler context
userStories.description: As a plugin developer, I want my tool's handle function to receive a context object with a reportProgress callback so that I can report incremental progress during long-running operations.
acceptanceCriteria: ToolDefinition.handle signature changes from handle(params) to handle(params, context?) where context is an optional second argument
ToolHandlerContext interface is exported from plugin-sdk with: { reportProgress(opts: { progress: number; total: number; message?: string }): void }
reportProgress is a synchronous void function (fire-and-forget from the tool's perspective)
Existing plugins that define handle(params) continue to work without changes (context is an optional second arg, not a breaking change)
The ToolDefinition generic types are preserved — handle still receives z.infer<TInput> as the first arg
defineTool() accepts tools with either handle(params) or handle(params, context) signatures
The context object is designed to be injected by the adapter runtime (US-002) — plugin-sdk only defines the types, not the runtime wiring
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes: Edit platform/plugin-sdk/src/index.ts. Add a ToolHandlerContext interface: { reportProgress(opts: { progress: number; total: number; message?: string }): void }. Export it. Change ToolDefinition.handle from handle(params: z.infer<TInput>): Promise<z.infer<TOutput>> to handle(params: z.infer<TInput>, context?: ToolHandlerContext): Promise<z.infer<TOutput>>. The context parameter is optional so existing plugins compile without changes. Update the plugin-sdk unit tests to verify both signatures are accepted by defineTool(). The ProgressOptions type ({ progress, total, message? }) should also be exported for plugin authors who want to type their progress data. Note: the adapter runtime (in the IIFE wrapper generated by opentabs-plugin build) will be responsible for constructing the context object and passing it to handle() — that wiring happens in US-002. After editing plugin-sdk, run bun run build from the root to verify all downstream packages (mcp-server, browser-extension, plugin-tools) still compile. Publish updated @opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk in US-005.

userStories.title: Wire progress from adapter through extension to server
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want progress notifications from tool handlers to flow from the browser adapter through the Chrome extension to the MCP server, which converts them to MCP ProgressNotifications, so that MCP clients receive real-time progress updates during tool execution.
acceptanceCriteria: Adapter runtime (IIFE wrapper in build.ts): when a tool is executed, the adapter constructs a ToolHandlerContext with a reportProgress callback and passes it as the second argument to handle()
reportProgress posts a structured message to the extension via window.postMessage or a similar mechanism that the content script can intercept
The progress message format from adapter to extension: { type: 'opentabs:progress', pluginName, toolName, dispatchId, progress, total, message? }
Extension background script: receives progress messages and forwards them to the MCP server over WebSocket as JSON-RPC notifications
Wire format from extension to server: { jsonrpc: '2.0', method: 'tool.progress', params: { dispatchId: string, progress: number, total: number, message?: string } }
MCP server (extension-protocol.ts): receives tool.progress notifications, looks up the pending dispatch by dispatchId, and emits an MCP ProgressNotification to the MCP client
MCP server maps tool.progress to the MCP SDK's notifications/progress format: { method: 'notifications/progress', params: { progressToken, progress, total, message? } }
The progressToken is obtained from the original tools/call request's _meta.progressToken field (MCP spec)
If the tools/call request did not include a progressToken, progress notifications from the tool are silently dropped (no error)
If the pending dispatch is not found (race condition: dispatch already resolved), the progress notification is silently dropped
Progress notifications are fire-and-forget — they do not block tool execution and errors in the progress chain do not affect the tool result
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes:
This story touches four components:

1. **Adapter runtime** (platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts — the wrapperCode template): The IIFE wrapper currently registers the plugin on globalThis.__openTabs.adapters with tools that have a handle(params) method. The executeToolOnTab function in tool-dispatch.ts calls tool.handle(tInput) in MAIN world via chrome.scripting.executeScript. The challenge: progress messages need to flow from the MAIN world script execution back to the extension *during* execution, not after. The approach: before calling tool.handle(), create a reportProgress callback that uses a BroadcastChannel, CustomEvent, or window.postMessage to send progress data. The extension's content script (or the executeToolOnTab wrapper itself) listens for these messages during the dispatch. Since executeToolOnTab uses chrome.scripting.executeScript with world: 'MAIN', the injected func runs in page context — it can use window.postMessage to send progress to the extension's content script listening in the ISOLATED world. However, the current architecture does NOT inject a persistent content script — adapters are injected as one-shot executeScript calls. Alternative approach: use chrome.runtime.sendMessage from the MAIN world — but this requires the extension's externally_connectable or messaging API. Simplest approach that works with CSP: the dispatchId is passed into the executeToolOnTab func, the func creates a BroadcastChannel(`opentabs-progress-${dispatchId}`), constructs the context with reportProgress posting to that channel, and the caller (handleToolDispatch in tool-dispatch.ts) listens on the same BroadcastChannel for the duration of the dispatch. BroadcastChannel works cross-context (MAIN to service worker) in Chrome MV3. Actually — BroadcastChannel does NOT work between MAIN world page scripts and the extension service worker (different origins). The correct approach: the executeToolOnTab func is already an async function injected via chrome.scripting.executeScript in MAIN world. It calls tool.handle(tInput) and returns the result. To support progress, we need to restructure: instead of returning only the final result, we need a side channel. The cleanest solution: use a MessageChannel/port pair — but chrome.scripting.executeScript doesn't support passing ports. The pragmatic solution matching the existing architecture: accumulate progress events on a global and have the extension poll, OR use window.postMessage from MAIN world and have a content script relay to the service worker. Given the platform already injects content scripts for adapters via chrome.scripting.registerContentScripts (in message-router.ts handleSyncFull), the best approach is: (a) In executeToolOnTab, before calling tool.handle(), define a reportProgress function that dispatches a CustomEvent on the document with a unique event name keyed by dispatchId. (b) Before executing the script, inject a separate content script (ISOLATED world) that listens for those CustomEvents and relays them to the service worker via chrome.runtime.sendMessage. (c) The service worker forwards the progress to the MCP server. Actually, CustomEvents fired in MAIN world ARE visible to ISOLATED world content scripts — they share the same DOM. This is the correct pattern.

Revised approach:
- In tool-dispatch.ts executeToolOnTab: the injected func receives the dispatchId as an additional arg. It creates a context object with reportProgress that fires document.dispatchEvent(new CustomEvent('opentabs:progress', { detail: { dispatchId, progress, total, message } })). It passes this context as the second arg to tool.handle(params, context).
- In tool-dispatch.ts handleToolDispatch: before calling executeToolOnTab, inject a temporary content script (ISOLATED world) via chrome.scripting.executeScript that listens for 'opentabs:progress' CustomEvents on the document and relays them to the service worker via chrome.runtime.sendMessage({ type: 'tool:progress', ... }). After the tool completes, inject another script to remove the listener (or use an AbortController). The listener script relays the CustomEvent detail to the background.
- In background.ts or message-router.ts: handle 'tool:progress' internal messages by forwarding them to the MCP server over WebSocket as { method: 'tool.progress', params: { dispatchId, progress, total, message? } }.
- In extension-protocol.ts handleExtensionMessage: handle 'tool.progress' method — look up the pending dispatch by dispatchId. Need to store the progressToken (from the MCP request's _meta) alongside the pending dispatch.
- In mcp-setup.ts: when handling tools/call, extract _meta.progressToken from the request and pass it through dispatchToExtension (or store it on the PendingDispatch). When tool.progress arrives, use the MCP server instance to send a ProgressNotification.
- In state.ts PendingDispatch: add an optional progressToken field and a reference to the MCP server session (to send the notification).

2. **Extension** (platform/browser-extension/src/tool-dispatch.ts): Modify executeToolOnTab to pass dispatchId and set up progress listener. Add progress relay logic.

3. **MCP Server** (platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts): Add handler for 'tool.progress' method in handleExtensionMessage.

4. **MCP Server** (platform/mcp-server/src/mcp-setup.ts): Extract progressToken from tools/call _meta, store on PendingDispatch, use it to emit MCP ProgressNotification via server.notification().

The dispatchId is already the JSON-RPC id used in the pending dispatch — reuse it as the correlation key.

userStories.title: Make dispatch timeout configurable and extend for progress-reporting tools
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want the dispatch timeout to reset each time a tool reports progress so that long-running tools that are actively working don't get killed by the 30s timeout.
acceptanceCriteria: When a tool.progress notification arrives for a pending dispatch, the dispatch timeout timer is cleared and reset to DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS from now
This means: as long as a tool reports progress at least once every 30 seconds, it will never time out
A new MAX_DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS constant (default 300_000 = 5 minutes) is added as an absolute upper bound
The timeout reset respects the max: if the dispatch has been running for more than MAX_DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS since startTs, the dispatch is rejected regardless of progress
PendingDispatch gains a lastProgressTs field (updated on each tool.progress) for debugging/observability
The default DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS (30s) remains unchanged for tools that don't report progress

The extension-side SCRIPT_TIMEOUT_MS in constants.ts is also extended when progress is being reported — or the extension timeout is removed for progress-reporting dispatches (since the server controls the authoritative timeout)
Unit tests in extension-protocol.test.ts verify: (a) timeout fires at 30s for tools without progress, (b) timeout resets on progress, (c) absolute max timeout fires even with continuous progress
bun run build passes
bun run type-check passes
bun run lint passes
bun run knip passes
bun run test passes
userStories.notes:
Edit platform/mcp-server/src/state.ts: Add MAX_DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS = 300_000. Add lastProgressTs?: number to PendingDispatch.

Edit platform/mcp-server/src/extension-protocol.ts: In the tool.progress handler (added in US-002), after emitting the MCP ProgressNotification, reset the dispatch timeout: clearTimeout(pending.timerId), set pending.lastProgressTs = Date.now(), compute remaining max time = MAX_DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS - (Date.now() - pending.startTs), if remaining > 0 set pending.timerId = setTimeout(rejectFn, Math.min(DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS, remaining)), else reject immediately.

For the extension side: The extension has its own SCRIPT_TIMEOUT_MS (25s, 5s less than server's 30s) in constants.ts. The executeToolOnTab function in tool-dispatch.ts uses Promise.race with a timeout. For progress-reporting tools, this extension-side timeout also needs to be extended. Options: (a) Remove the extension timeout entirely and let the server be the authority — risky, as a hung script could block the tab forever. (b) Have the extension also reset its timeout when it sees a progress event from the content script listener. Option (b) is better. Modify executeToolOnTab to accept an AbortSignal or timeout-reset callback. When a progress event arrives (from the content script listener set up in US-002), reset the extension-side timeout too. Add a MAX_SCRIPT_TIMEOUT_MS (5 minutes, matching server max) as the extension-side absolute bound.

Update extension-protocol.test.ts: Add test cases for timeout reset behavior. Use jest fake timers to simulate: (1) dispatch without progress → times out at 30s, (2) dispatch with progress at 20s → timeout resets, resolves at 50s, (3) dispatch with continuous progress every 20s → times out at MAX (5 min).

userStories.title: Add E2E tests for progress reporting
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want E2E tests that verify the full progress notification chain from adapter to MCP client so that we have confidence the feature works end-to-end.
acceptanceCriteria: A new tool slow_with_progress is added to the e2e-test plugin that sleeps for a configurable duration and reports progress at regular intervals
slow_with_progress input: { durationMs: number, intervalMs: number, steps: number } — reports `steps` progress notifications at `intervalMs` intervals while working for `durationMs` total
E2E test: invoke slow_with_progress with durationMs=3000, steps=3 — verify that 3 progress notifications are received by the MCP client before the final result
E2E test: invoke slow_with_progress with durationMs=35000, steps=5 — verify the tool completes successfully despite exceeding the 30s default timeout (progress resets the timer)
E2E test: verify progress notifications contain correct progress, total, and message fields
E2E test: verify that a tool WITHOUT progress still times out at 30s (existing behavior preserved)
The e2e-test plugin is rebuilt with the updated plugin-sdk that includes ToolHandlerContext
bun run test:e2e passes
userStories.notes:
Edit plugins/e2e-test/src/tools/ — add a new slow_with_progress.ts tool file. The tool takes { durationMs, intervalMs, steps } as input. In handle(params, context), it loops `steps` times, calling context?.reportProgress({ progress: i+1, total: steps, message: `Step ${i+1} of ${steps}` }) and then awaiting a sleep(intervalMs). After all steps, it returns { completed: true, stepsReported: steps }.

Rebuild the e2e-test plugin: cd plugins/e2e-test && bun install (to pick up updated @opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk) && bun run build.

Add E2E test file e2e/progress.e2e.ts (or add tests to an existing file). The MCP client in the E2E fixture needs to capture progress notifications. The MCP SDK's Client class supports onprogress callback or event handling — check how the fixture's callTool works and add progress capture. The Streamable HTTP transport supports receiving server-sent notifications inline during a request.

For the 35s test: this test will take >35s to run. Mark it with a longer timeout in Playwright (test.setTimeout(60_000)). The key assertion: the tool succeeds (no timeout error) despite running longer than DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS.

For the no-progress timeout test: use the existing e2e-test plugin's echo tool or add a slow_no_progress tool that sleeps for 35s without reporting progress — verify it times out with the DISPATCH_TIMEOUT_MS error.

Note: the MCP client must send a progressToken in the tools/call request's _meta for the server to emit progress. The E2E fixture's callTool helper may need updating to include _meta: { progressToken: <unique-id> } and register a progress handler.

userStories.title: Publish updated platform packages and update plugins
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want to publish the updated @opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk (with ToolHandlerContext) and @opentabs-dev/shared (if changed) so that the e2e-test plugin and external plugins can use the new progress API.
acceptanceCriteria: Publish packages in dependency order: shared (if changed) → plugin-sdk → plugin-tools (if changed)
Wait for npm propagation before updating downstream dependencies
Update e2e-test plugin dependencies to the new versions
Rebuild e2e-test plugin: bun install && bun run build
All quality gates pass: bun run build, type-check, lint, knip, test
userStories.notes:
Use scripts/publish.sh <version> to publish. The plugin-sdk is the primary package that changes (new ToolHandlerContext export). If shared types were modified (unlikely for this feature), publish shared first. plugin-tools may need a publish if the IIFE wrapper template changed (it does in US-002 — the build.ts wrapperCode template needs to be aware of the context injection pattern, though the actual runtime injection happens in the extension's executeToolOnTab, not in the IIFE itself). Actually — the IIFE wrapper in build.ts just registers the plugin; the context injection happens at dispatch time in the extension's chrome.scripting.executeScript call. So plugin-tools may not need changes. Verify.

After publishing, update plugins/e2e-test/package.json to reference the new @opentabs-dev/plugin-sdk version, run bun install, rebuild.

userStories.title: Update CLAUDE.md for streaming progress support
userStories.description: As a platform developer, I want CLAUDE.md to document the progress reporting capability so that future development understands the progress notification chain and its constraints.
acceptanceCriteria: Key Concepts section mentions progress reporting for long-running tools
The dispatch chain description includes progress as a backward notification path
The timeout behavior (reset on progress, absolute max) is documented
The ToolHandlerContext API (reportProgress) is mentioned in the plugin development section
The wire format for tool.progress is documented in the architecture section
No new sections are added for this — information is integrated into existing sections where relevant
userStories.notes: Edit CLAUDE.md. In the Architecture section, add a note about the bidirectional nature of the dispatch chain (tool results flow backward, progress notifications also flow backward during execution). In Key Concepts, add a 'Progress reporting' paragraph explaining: tools can report progress via context.reportProgress(), progress flows adapter → extension → server → MCP client, dispatch timeout resets on each progress message, absolute max timeout of 5 minutes. In the Adding a New Plugin section or near the tool definition pattern, mention that handle() now accepts an optional second context argument with reportProgress. Keep additions concise — this is incremental documentation, not a new major section.
